title: "1,000 days left" Anthropic founder
summary: A YouTube video by Wes Roth discussing Jack Clark's commentary on Anthropic and a quoted reference to "1,000 days left" attributed to an Anthropic founder. The exact context and substance of the claim is not detailed in the provided text.
writeup: Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark puts a 60-plus percent probability on fully automated AI R&D, where AI systems build their own successors without human involvement, arriving by end of 2028. The video unpacks why this threshold is tractable: AI research is largely code, text, and math with verifiable outputs, exactly the domain LLMs already occupy, with AlphaFold as a concrete deployed instance of an LLM ensemble autonomously searching for better algorithms. Running in parallel, Google DeepMind hired Alex Imas as director of AGI economics (working with co-founder Shane Legg) to formally prepare for the disruption to the labor-for-resources system that has organized human economies since the hunter-gatherer era. The through-line is that frontier lab insiders are no longer treating this transition as speculative; they are staffing, writing essays, and assigning job titles to grapple with implications they describe as nearly impossible to forecast.
key_points: Jack Clark, Anthropic co-founder and former head of policy at OpenAI, assigns a 60-plus percent probability to fully automated AI R&D, meaning an AI system capable of building its own successor without any human involvement, arriving by the end of 2028.

Clark describes this as a reluctant conclusion whose implications he feels dwarfed by, calls crossing this threshold a Rubicon into a nearly unforecastable future, and says he plans to spend most of 2026 working through the implications publicly.

The tractability argument: AI research reduces to hypothesis generation, code writing, and evaluation against verifiable metrics, which is squarely within what LLMs already do, making the domain unlike physical sciences where wet-lab manipulation gates progress.

AlphaFold demonstrates the pattern in practice: a Gemini-family LLM sits at the center of a harness with prompt samplers, evaluator pools, and a program database searching for the best algorithm, with multiple LLMs in ensemble depending on the subtask, and the key enabling condition is that outputs are verifiable.

Google DeepMind hired Alex Imas as director of AGI economics, working with co-founder Shane Legg, to formally prepare for how frontier AI will reshape wealth, labor, and markets, because the fundamental labor-for-resources system organizing human economies since the hunter-gatherer era faces disruption from AGI.

The Meter Research capability-tracking chart, updated with the Claude Mytos preview, shows a steep upward inflection that matches the intelligence explosion curve Wait But Why predicted, making the trajectory look empirical rather than theoretical.
